prompt: A traditional Japanese woodblock print, Ukiyo-e style, late Edo Period. The composition utilizes strict isometric projection and stacked spatial logic rather than linear perspective, creating a towering, steep, multi-terraced mountain face blanketed in deep snow. A restricted historical palette of indigo blue, Prussian blue, cinnabar vermilion, yellow ochre, and stark white is used. A lone, weary traveler wearing a traditional straw hat and straw rain cape navigates the precarious downhill path. This traveler leaves a very distinct, discrete trail of dark footprints pressed deeply into the white snow, marking each specific downward step. These steps clearly zigzag downhill, decreasing in length as they descend. Bold black sumi ink outlines define every form, filled with flat, unmodulated blocks of color. At the absolute lowest point of the ravine, at the base of the towering mountain, nestled in the deep shadows, is a single, humble, rustic tea house with a single small, intensely glowing orange paper lantern. Visible textures of aged washi paper and the faint pattern of the wood grain pressing through the ink are present across the surface. No text, no calligraphic characters, no mathematical notation, no floating labels, and no modern overlays exist within the image.
tradition_approach: Utilization of isometric projection and stacked perspective rather than linear depth. Restricted historical palette (Indigo, Vermilion, Ochre). Thick, bold outlines defining flat blocks of unmodulated color. Visible washi paper and wood grain textures. No text overlays.
